title: What Does My Work Give My Children?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for mothers who work and carry the guilt of it
excerpt: A set of six journal prompts for working mothers to examine what their work gives their children, not only what it takes. The prompts move from a broad orienting question toward specific, overlooked contributions.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the working-mum guilt has been running all week and you want to write your way to a different view of it.

content.text: A journal prompt set for working mothers. The guilt focuses on what work takes. These prompts ask about what work gives, in the hope of putting a more complete picture on paper.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
What does the guilt actually say I'm doing wrong?
Write the guilt's version of the story. Get it on paper so you can look at it clearly.
Where did that version of the story come from?
A parent, a school-gate comment, a cultural idea about what good mothers do?
Closer in
What does my work give my children that I tend to overlook?
Name it specifically. Financial security, a model of independence, a parent who is not depleted by staying home, a sense that work matters.
What might they say about my work when they are adults?
Write the version of it they might tell their own children. Not the guilt's version. Yours.
What would change if I let the guilt be a cultural script rather than evidence?
Write what you would do differently this week if you treated the guilt as information about social pressure rather than proof of failure.
What does working give me, that I then bring home?
Write what you have when you come home that you wouldn't have if you hadn't gone.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education.
